does: This paper proposes GradeSQL, a test-time inference method that leverages Outcome Reward Models (ORMs) to improve text-to-SQL generation from large language models. Rather than relying on surface-level heuristics like syntactic correctness or frequency-based selection, the approach trains ORMs to assign utility scores to generated SQL queries based on semantic correctness. During inference, when an LLM produces multiple candidate SQL queries, the ORM selects the best one by scoring outputs for their actual semantic validity. The paper demonstrates that this ORM-based selection outperforms existing test-time strategies including Best-of-N execution-based selection and Majority Voting, particularly on complex SQL generation tasks where multiple syntactically valid but semantically incorrect queries are common.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in test-time inference strategies for LLM-based code and SQL generation, extending beyond earlier execution-based validation and voting mechanisms. It connects to the growing literature on Outcome Reward Models developed in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) contexts, adapting these semantic evaluation techniques to the text-to-SQL domain. The paper responds to limitations in existing Best-of-N and Majority Voting approaches by introducing a learned semantic evaluator rather than heuristic-based selection. It also builds on prior work exploring how LLMs can be improved through multiple sampling and intelligent selection, while acknowledging that current methods fail on complex queries despite advances from instruction-tuned models.
enables: This work opens directions for applying learned reward models more broadly to code generation and database query tasks. It suggests future research into improving ORM training specifically for SQL semantics, potentially using execution results, schema information, and query equivalence checking. The approach could extend to other structured prediction tasks where multiple valid solutions exist or where semantic correctness is difficult to assess from syntax alone. Additionally, the paper points toward combining ORMs with other test-time strategies, iterative refinement approaches, or fine-tuning methods to further boost complex query generation. It also motivates developing better reward signals and training procedures for ORMs in specialized domains, and exploring how such semantic scorers could enable more efficient sampling or active query generation.
